---
title: "Collaborative Diagramming with AI: From MCP Export to Screenshot-Driven Iteration"
description: "How a draw.io MCP server's limitations led to a desktop-app workflow where human and AI edit the same diagram through XML manipulation and screenshot feedback"
date: 2026-03-06
categories:
  - code-explorations
---

Diagramming with an AI assistant should be straightforward: describe what you want, get
a diagram, iterate. In practice, the tooling for this is immature. After working through
three different approaches to draw.io integration with Claude Code, the one that stuck
was also the least obvious — direct XML editing with screenshot-based visual feedback.

## The starting point: draw.io MCP server

The [draw.io MCP server](https://github.com/italomaia/drawio-mcp) gives Claude tools to
create and manipulate diagrams programmatically. On paper, this is the ideal integration
— structured API calls that produce diagram files.

The problem is that the MCP is essentially a one-way export pipe. Claude can create a
diagram and dump it to a `.drawio` file, but there's no mechanism for collaborative
iteration. The human can't make changes in a GUI and have Claude pick up those changes.
Claude can't see what the diagram actually looks like after rendering. The workflow is:

1. Claude generates a diagram via MCP tools
2. Human opens the file, looks at it
3. Human describes what's wrong in natural language
4. Claude regenerates, blind to the actual visual result
5. Repeat

This works for trivial diagrams but breaks down quickly for anything that requires
spatial reasoning — node positioning, alignment, edge routing, visual balance. Claude is
generating XML coordinates it can never see the result of.

## The web UI detour

The draw.io web application seemed like it could close the loop. The idea was to use the
web UI as the shared visual surface — Claude edits the underlying XML, the human views
and edits in the browser.

Two problems killed this approach. First, the same fundamental issue: there was no
efficient way for Claude to see the rendered diagram. The human would have to describe
the visual state or take manual screenshots and feed them back.

Second, the web UI itself was unstable for this kind of workflow. It constantly popped up
alerts about unsaved changes — every external file modification triggered a disruptive
modal dialog. When the iteration loop involves frequent file writes from outside the
browser, this becomes untenable. The UI fought the workflow instead of supporting it.

## The solution: desktop app + screenshots

The approach that works uses the draw.io desktop application with a screenshot-based
feedback loop:

1. Claude creates or edits the `.drawio` XML file directly using file editing tools
2. The human opens or reloads the file in the draw.io desktop app
3. Claude captures a screenshot of the draw.io window using macOS `screencapture`
4. Claude reads the screenshot image to assess the visual result
5. Claude makes targeted XML edits, and the loop repeats

The key enabler is background window capture. A small Swift script finds the draw.io
window by ID, and `screencapture -l <windowID>` grabs it without bringing it to the
foreground — no need to switch windows or interrupt whatever else is happening on screen.

```bash
# Find the draw.io window ID
swift find_drawio_window.swift
# Output: 12345|mydiagram.drawio

# Capture without foregrounding
screencapture -l 12345 -x /tmp/drawio_screenshot.png
```

This gives both human and AI shared visibility into the diagram's actual state. Claude
can see whether nodes are properly aligned, whether edge routing makes sense, whether
labels are readable. The human can make manual adjustments in the GUI, save, and Claude
picks up those changes by re-reading the XML before its next edit.

## What this workflow actually looks like

A typical iteration:

1. "Draw a system architecture diagram with three services and a message queue"
2. Claude writes the `.drawio` XML — explicit coordinates for every shape, edge, label
3. Human opens the file. Claude screenshots and sees the result.
4. "The queue node overlaps the auth service. Move it down and add a label to the edge."
5. Claude reads the current XML (the human may have tweaked something), edits the
   coordinates, writes the file
6. Human reloads. Claude screenshots. Checks alignment, adjusts.

The `.drawio` format works well for this because everything is explicit — unlike Mermaid
or PlantUML, where an auto-layout engine decides positioning, draw.io XML specifies
exact x/y coordinates, dimensions, and edge waypoints. Claude has full spatial control.

## The trade-off

The main inconvenience is the explicit sync step. After Claude edits the file, the human
needs to reload in draw.io. The desktop app sometimes shows a yellow "file modified"
banner that triggers a reload on click; other times the file needs to be closed and
reopened. There's no File > Revert in draw.io desktop, which is a minor irritant.

Going the other direction — human edits to Claude — requires the human to save in the
GUI before Claude reads the file. draw.io can silently reformat the XML on save
(reordering attributes, changing whitespace), so Claude needs to re-read the full file
rather than assuming its last-written version is current.

These are real friction points, but they're predictable and manageable. The alternative
approaches had friction that was either invisible (Claude generating blind) or actively
hostile (web UI fighting external edits).

## Takeaways

The MCP approach optimized for the wrong thing — programmatic access to diagram creation
rather than collaborative iteration. The value isn't in generating a diagram; it's in
refining one together, which requires shared visual state.

Screenshot-based feedback is an underappreciated pattern for AI tool integration. Any
desktop application becomes a collaborative surface if the AI can capture and read its
window. The approach generalizes beyond draw.io to any GUI tool where the AI manipulates
underlying files while observing rendered output.

The explicit sync step, while mildly inconvenient, enforces a useful discipline: both
parties always work from the same saved state. There's no drift between what Claude
thinks the diagram looks like and what the human sees. That alignment turns out to matter
more than seamless automatic sync would.
